content.full_text:
Flrn Pollrrcal PnacrrcBs CouurssroN
P.O. Box lJ07 . 128 J Street . Sucrarnenro. CA 95l.itl-01J07
(916) -122--5660 . Fax (916) 322-01J86
September 7,2000
Mr. Steven G. Standerfer
Antelope Valley College
3041 W. Ave. K
Lancaster, CA 93536-5426
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-193
Dear Mr. Standerfer:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the mass mailing
prohibition in the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Your letter does not indicate whether you
have been authorized by the Lancaster City Council to request advice on their behalf, thus, we
decline to advise you with respect to the city's newsletter. (Reg. 18329O)(8)(B) and (c)(+)(C).)
Moreover, your other questions appear to be general in nature, thus we are keating these
questions as requests for informal assistance. (Reg. 1S329(bX8XC) and (c).) Informal assistance
does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Section
831 14; Reg. 1 8329(cX3).)
QUESTIONS
1. "If an elected official within the agency merely asks for the names of all elected
agency officials to be included in all mass mailings, is this acceptable or is this considered acting
in concert with the official? For instance, if I work for an agency where elected officials want to
have their names added to all brochures - brochures that are frequently mass mailed to people -
can the names of the elected officials appear, even though no other representatives of the agerr"y
are included?"
2' "[I]s there anything that would bar the use of elected officials' names prominently on
the front cover of a publication, so long as the headlines, type size, color, etc. are in keeping with
the rest of the publication that is mass mailed?"
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commissionregulations appear attitle2,sections 18109 - 18996, of
the California Code of Regulations.


File No. I-00-193
PageZ
DISCUSSION
Section 89001 of the Act provides: 'T.{o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." (Section 89001.) Regulation 18901 interprets and implements the prohibition
in section 89001. Regulation 18901(a) prohibits a mailing if all of the following four criteria are
met (absent an express exception in subdivision (b) of the regulation).
1. The item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place
of employment or business, or post office box; and the item is a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document. 
,
2. The item sent either:
(a) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer, or
(b) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends
the mailing.
3. Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or the costs of design,
production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with public moneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted.
4. More than 200 substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request. @eg. 18901(a).)
However, even when these four criteria are met, the mass mailing may still be
permissible if an exception in regulation 18901(b) applies. For example, subdivision (b)(1) of
the regulation provides that: "Any item in which the elected officer's nirme appears only in the
letterhead or logotype of the stationery, forms (including'?or Your Information" or
"Compliments of'cards), and envelopes of the agency sending the mailing, or of a committee of
the agency, or of the elected officer, or in a roster listing containing the names of all elected
officers of the agency. In any such item, the names of all elected officers must appear in the
same type size, typeface, type color, and location. Such item may not include the elected
officer's photograph, signafure, or any other reference to the elected officer, except as
specifically permitted..." in some other exception in regulation (b). (^lee, Reg. 18901, enclosed,
for a complete list of exceptions.)


File No. I-00-193
Page 3
In response to your specific questions:
1. "If an elected official within the agency merely asks for the names of all elected
agency officials to be included in all mass mailings, is this acceptable or is this considered acting
in concert with the official? For instance, if I work for an agency where elected officials want to
have their names added to all brochures - brochures that are frequently mass mailed to people -
can the names of the elected officials appear, even though no other representatives of the agency
are included?"
The regulation prohibits reference to the name, office, or any other reference to an elected
officer (or the inclusion of a photograph), who is affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing under two circumstances: (a) If the mailing is prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer, (b) or where the mailing features
the elected officer affiliated with the agency, irrespective if the mailing was prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer or not. In either of
these circumstances, the prohibition will apply absent an exception.
Where the elected officer requests the inclusion of his or her name, the mailing is sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer and the elected
officer's name may not be included in a mass mailing, absent an express exception. (Stone
Advice Letter, No. I-89-670.)
2. "[I]s there anything that would bar the use of elected officials' names prominently on
the front cover of a publication, so long as the headlines, type size, color, etc. are in keeping with
the rest of the publication that is mass mailed?"
You have not provided sufficient facts for us to adequately answer this question.
However, we can make the following general statements with respect to the characteristics of the
appearance of an official's name in a mailing.
o If the mailing in question is not a mass mailing because it does not meet the criteria set
forth in regulation 18901(a), then the Act would not restrict the characteristics of the
printed name of the elected official (such as type size and color).
. If the mailing is a mass mailing pursuant to regulation 18901(a) and the mailing is sent
in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer, the
name may not appear at all, absent an exception.
o If the mailing is a mass mailing pursuant to regulation 18901(a) and the mailing is not
sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer, the
name may appear so long as it is not featured. Regulation 18901(c)(2) provides:
" 'Features an elected officer' means that the item mailed includes the elected officer's


File No. I-00-193
Page 4
photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by the manner of display of
his or lter name or ffice in the layout of the document, such as by headlines, captions,
type size, typeface, or type color." [Emphasis added]. Thus, if the item mailed includes
the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by the
manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as
by headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color, the mailing would be
prohibited.
I hope this general discussion of the law is helpful. We have enclosed a copy of
regulation 18901 for your information. If you have any other questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
,
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
John W. Wallace
Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enc. 18901.

(a) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in

3. Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or the costs of design,
production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with public moneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted.
4. More than 200 substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,

Assistant General Counsel
John W. Wallace
Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division
